meta.title: Neurosurgery Practice Software | Surgical Coordination & Post-Op RTM | clinIQ
meta.description: Complete guide to neurosurgery practice operations. Complex case coordination. LOMN generation in 5 minutes. Post-operative RTM revenue. OR efficiency for brain and spine cases.

hero.subhead: Complex cases need systematic coordination. Surgical authorizations need LOMN templates that take 5 minutes, not 45. Post-operative patients need monitoring that captures RTM revenue. clinIQ handles neurosurgery's operational complexity — from tumor board to OR to recovery.

sections.content:
Neurosurgery practices manage some of medicine's most complex cases — brain tumors, complex spinal pathology, vascular malformations, movement disorders requiring deep brain stimulation. The clinical complexity is matched by an extraordinary administrative burden that consumes hours every day.

The average neurosurgical practice has:

• Cases requiring 6-8 pre-operative workup steps before OR scheduling can begin
• Prior authorization denial rates of 25-35% for complex spine procedures on first submission
• LOMN generation consuming 45-90 minutes per case across coordinators and surgeons
• OR setup coordination spanning implants, neuromonitoring, neuronavigation, and fluoroscopy — all confirmed separately
• Post-operative monitoring extending weeks to months for major cranial and complex spine cases

Most neurosurgical practices manage this coordination through manual tracking — whiteboards, spreadsheets, and staff memory. The result: cases delayed by missing clearances, authorizations expiring before surgery is scheduled, and post-operative patients whose recovery generates no RTM revenue because nobody is capturing it.

When a 45-minute LOMN takes 5 minutes with the right template, and post-op RTM generates $8,500-12,000 per month in previously uncaptured revenue, the operational investment pays for itself in the first month.

Neurosurgery cases don't start in the OR — they start weeks earlier with a coordination sequence that determines whether the case happens on time, with the right team, and without last-minute delays.

**Tumor cases require:**

Neuroradiology review and formal consultation scheduling. Tumor board presentation preparation and attendance coordination. Oncology and radiation oncology input for combined modality planning. Awake craniotomy planning with neuropsychology (language and motor mapping). Functional MRI and diffusion tensor tractography ordering and review. Intraoperative neurophysiology monitoring arrangement — SSEP, MEP, language mapping.

**Complex spine cases require:**

MRI and CT correlation to confirm fusion levels. Pain management coordination for patients with established chronic pain management. Physical therapy pre-operative optimization for frail or deconditioned patients. Medical clearance — cardiology for high-risk patients, pulmonology for patients with respiratory compromise. Implant representative scheduling — pedicle screws, interbody cages, motion-preserving devices — all confirmed with case-specific sizes and backups.

**What clinIQ does:**

Case pipeline tracking: Every case in the practice pipeline visible from referral to scheduled OR date. Pre-op checklist: Each required step tracked to completion with responsible party assigned. Bottleneck identification: Case stalled at neuroradiology review for 10 days → flag for coordinator action. Calendar coordination: When all pre-op steps complete, OR scheduling coordinated across surgeon, hospital, and team. Implant ordering: Case-specific implant requirements tracked and confirmed pre-surgery.

**Related: [Neurosurgery Case Coordination: Managing Complex Pre-Op Workflows](/blog/neurosurgery-case-coordination)**

Every neurosurgical authorization requires a Letter of Medical Necessity. For complex cases, that letter must document the entire clinical picture: diagnosis, failed conservative treatment, imaging findings, neurological examination, and specific indications for the requested procedure. A well-written LOMN is the difference between first-submission approval and a 60-day appeal cycle.

**What makes neurosurgery LOMNs complex:**

Radiology correlation: 'L4-5 disc herniation with 8mm posterior protrusion correlating with clinical L5 radiculopathy confirmed on EMG' — requires pulling and synthesizing multiple reports into a coherent narrative.

Conservative care documentation: Most payers require 6+ weeks of failed conservative care for elective spine procedures. Documenting this requires reviewing the complete patient history across all treating providers.

Step therapy compliance: Many payers require specific sequences — epidural steroid injections before fusion, medial branch blocks before radiofrequency ablation, SCS trial before permanent implant. Each step must be documented with date, outcome, and reason for progression.

**LOMN templates in clinIQ for neurosurgery:**

• Anterior cervical discectomy and fusion (ACDF)
• Cervical disc arthroplasty
• Lumbar discectomy (microdiscectomy)
• Lumbar fusion (TLIF, PLIF, ALIF, lateral)
• Cervical and lumbar laminectomy/laminotomy
• Spinal cord stimulator trial and permanent implant
• Deep brain stimulation (Parkinson's, essential tremor, dystonia)
• Craniotomy for tumor resection (supratentorial, posterior fossa)
• Ventriculoperitoneal and ventriculoatrial shunt
• Peripheral nerve decompression (cubital tunnel, carpal tunnel)

**Auto-population from patient record:**

Diagnosis codes, imaging findings (pulled from radiology reports), neurological examination findings, conservative care history, prior procedure history. Surgeon reviews, edits case-specific details, signs. Five minutes instead of forty-five.

**Time savings:** For a practice generating 30 LOMNs per month: 20 hours per month recovered — more than $3,000/month in coordinator time.

**Related: [Neurosurgery Prior Authorization: Reducing Complex Spine Denials](/blog/neurosurgery-prior-auth)**

Neurosurgery patients have some of medicine's most intensive post-operative monitoring needs — and the longest recovery periods. A craniotomy patient may require 3-6 months of recovery monitoring. A complex spine fusion patient requires 6-12 months. A spinal cord stimulator patient continues monitoring indefinitely. This extended recovery window is an RTM billing opportunity that most neurosurgical practices don't capture.

**RTM qualification for neurosurgery post-op:**

Neurological symptom monitoring qualifies as non-physiological remote monitoring under RTM codes. Specifically:

Headache patterns — location, severity, character, relationship to position. Visual changes — blurring, diplopia, visual field deficits. Cognitive symptoms — memory, concentration, processing speed. Focal neurological deficits — weakness, numbness, coordination. Wound healing — incision status, drainage, signs of CSF leak. Activity progression — physical therapy milestones, return-to-activity criteria. Medication compliance — anticonvulsants post-craniotomy, steroids, pain medications.

**The CPT codes:**

• 98975: RTM device supply and setup (one-time)
• 98977: Monthly device supply with scheduled recordings
• 98980: Treatment management, first 20 minutes (monthly)
• 98981: Each additional 20 minutes

**The billing math:**

65 active post-operative patients × $130/month average reimbursement = $8,450/month. Annual: $101,400. For practices with higher surgical volume, proportionally more.

**Patient engagement:**

Brain surgery and complex spine surgery patients are highly motivated. They're concerned about neurological recovery. They want structured communication. Enrollment rates above 80% are typical when RTM is presented as part of the surgical care program.

**What clinIQ captures:**

Post-op symptom checklist: Daily tracking during acute recovery, weekly during subacute, monthly during maintenance. Red flag alerts: New headache plus nausea post-craniotomy flags as potential elevated ICP — triggers provider notification. Recovery milestone tracking: Return of neurological function documented with timestamps for medical-legal documentation. Provider review dashboard: Which patients need attention this week based on symptom trends and time since surgery.

Neurosurgery OR cases are expensive and long. An 8-hour complex spine fusion occupies an OR all day. A craniotomy consumes 6-10 hours of premium OR time. OR efficiency — maximizing case volume within available OR time — directly determines neurosurgical revenue. The difference between a prepared OR and an unprepared one can mean the difference between completing 4 cases or 3.

**The neurosurgery OR efficiency problem:**

Case delays are common and costly:

Missing implants: Pedicle screw system confirmed, but the iliac crest harvest tray not included in the instrument set. Case delayed 45 minutes.

Neuromonitoring setup: Monitoring company not notified of baseline monitoring requirements. Setup extended 30 minutes into scheduled OR time.

First case delays: Patient arrives at 7:10, surgical consent not yet signed, anesthesia pre-op not completed. 7:30 case starts at 8:15.

Missing imaging: Neuronavigation registration requires MRI sequences that weren't ordered with the correct protocols. Case proceeds without navigation or is delayed for rescanning.

**Pre-operative preparation checklist in clinIQ:**

• Imaging: MRI sequences in neuronavigation system, CT if needed for registration
• Implants: Case-specific sizes confirmed with representative, backups ordered
• Intraoperative monitoring: Team briefed on case requirements, lead placement planned
• Anesthesia: Airway plan, positioning requirements, TCI if applicable
• Surgical consent: Signed, on chart, with specific procedure documented
• Medical clearance: All clearances obtained, significant issues communicated to anesthesia
• Blood: Type and screen complete, blood products ordered for high-bleeding-risk cases

**Turnover tracking:**

For practices doing multiple shorter cases per day — carpal tunnel, lumbar microdiscectomy, occipital nerve stimulator — turnover efficiency matters. clinIQ tracks room-ready to patient-in time, identifying where minutes are lost.

**First-case on-time starts:**

Every 30-minute first-case delay on a complex spine day costs a slot that may not be recoverable. First-case on-time start rate tracked and reported weekly.

Neurosurgery has among the highest prior authorization complexity in medicine. Insurance companies scrutinize high-cost procedures aggressively. Complex spine fusions, spinal cord stimulators, and DBS require extensive documentation — and often face initial denial regardless of clear clinical indication.

**Common neurosurgery authorization challenges:**

Step therapy for spine: Most payers require documented epidural steroid injections before fusion authorization. Some require two ESI series with specific intervals. Documentation of each injection — date, treating provider, dose, patient response — must be referenced in the LOMN.

Conservative care documentation: '6 weeks of failed PT' must be specifically documented — dates of physical therapy, treating therapist, objective measures used, reason treatment was discontinued as inadequate.

Imaging correlation requirements: The specific operative level must correlate with imaging and clinical symptoms. 'Multilevel degenerative disc disease' is insufficient. 'L4-5 disc herniation with loss of disc height, Modic Type II endplate changes, and clinical L5 radiculopathy confirmed by EMG' is what authorization reviewers need.

Revision surgery documentation: Prior operative report, specific reason for revision, and documentation that the revision does not represent a complication of prior surgery.

**Spinal cord stimulator authorization complexity:**

SCS requires: psychological evaluation confirming patient is appropriate candidate, failed medication trials (specific agents, doses, duration documented), failed interventional procedures (epidurals, nerve blocks), positive response to diagnostic blocks. Trial period requires separate authorization from permanent implant.

**What clinIQ tracks:**

Authorization status: Every pending auth visible — payer, procedure, submission date, status, expected decision date. Conservative care log: Physical therapy and injection history maintained and referenced automatically. Step therapy compliance: System flags when required documentation steps are incomplete before submission. Denial management: Reason captured, appeal initiated with pre-built template incorporating denial rationale.

**Related: [Spine Surgery Prior Authorization: Step Therapy and Documentation Requirements](/blog/spine-surgery-prior-auth)**

**Week 1-2: Configuration and Setup**

LOMN templates built for your specific surgical procedures. Post-operative RTM program configured for your patient population. OR preparation checklist built to your facility's workflow. Authorization tracking configured for your primary payers.

**Week 3: Training and Go-Live**

Coordinator training on case pipeline management and LOMN generation. Surgical scheduling team trained on OR prep checklist. PA and APP trained on RTM review dashboard. Surgeon dashboard configured for authorization and case pipeline visibility.

**ROI Timeline:**

Month 1: LOMN time savings begin immediately — 45-minute process becomes 5 minutes. First RTM billing cycle initiated. OR prep checklist reduces first-case delays.

Month 3: 50+ post-op patients enrolled in RTM. Authorization approval rate tracking shows improvement. First full quarter of RTM billing generating $25,000+.

Month 6: Steady-state RTM generating $8,500-12,000/month. LOMN turnaround at 5 minutes. Documented reduction in authorization denials and case delays.

**Annual ROI:**

• Post-op RTM: $101,000+/year (65 patients)
• LOMN time savings: 240 hours/year at coordinator cost
• Recovered authorization denials: $30,000-80,000/year depending on payer mix

**Investment:**

• Professional: $499/month — Full suite including RTM, LOMN generation, case coordination, OR prep, analytics
• Implementation: $750 one-time
• EHR integration available for eClinicalWorks, Athenahealth, Epic

faqs.q: What LOMN templates do you have for neurosurgery?
faqs.a: clinIQ includes templates for ACDF, cervical disc arthroplasty, lumbar discectomy, lumbar fusion (TLIF/PLIF/ALIF/lateral), laminectomy, spinal cord stimulator trial and permanent, deep brain stimulation, craniotomy for tumor, VP/VA shunt, and peripheral nerve decompression. Each template auto-populates from patient data — imaging findings, conservative care history, diagnosis codes — and surgeon reviews and signs.

faqs.q: Is post-operative RTM covered by Medicare and commercial payers?
faqs.a: Yes. Medicare and most commercial payers cover RTM under CPT 98975, 98977, 98980, and 98981 for non-physiological monitoring including symptom tracking. Post-operative neurosurgery patients qualify under musculoskeletal and neurological monitoring. Reimbursement averages $120-150 per patient per month depending on payer mix.

faqs.q: How does the case coordination system work?
faqs.a: Each surgical case has a pipeline view showing all required pre-operative steps with completion status. Missing steps are highlighted. Cases stalled at a specific step for more than a defined period flag automatically. The system assigns responsible parties to each step and tracks completion — imaging ordered, clearances obtained, implants confirmed, consent scheduled. When all steps complete, OR scheduling is initiated.

faqs.q: How do you handle spinal cord stimulator authorization?
faqs.a: SCS authorization tracking includes the specific documentation requirements: psychological evaluation completion, failed medication trial log (agent, dose, duration, outcome), failed procedural interventions, diagnostic block response documentation. The LOMN template incorporates all required elements. Trial authorization and permanent implant authorization tracked separately.

Yes. clinIQ tracks first-case on-time start rate, room-ready to patient-in time, turnover time between cases, and pre-operative checklist completion rate. For practices doing multiple cases per day, bottleneck identification shows where time is lost — implants, anesthesia pre-op, consent — so the specific issue can be addressed.

faqs.q: What's the typical ROI for a neurosurgery practice?
faqs.a: Most neurosurgical practices see ROI in the first month. Post-op RTM with 65 patients generates $101,000/year. LOMN time savings across 30 cases per month recovers 20+ hours of coordinator time. Authorization denial reductions and faster approvals reduce case delays. Combined, most practices see $150,000-200,000 in annual benefit against a $750/year implementation and $6,000/year subscription cost.

faqs.q: Does clinIQ integrate with our EHR?
faqs.a: Yes. Integrations available for eClinicalWorks (bidirectional), Athenahealth, Allscripts, and Epic (custom). Patient data, appointment schedules, and relevant clinical data for LOMN generation flow automatically. RTM documentation pushes back to the EHR where supported. clinIQ can also operate standalone without integration.

Two weeks from contract to go-live. Week 1: workflow mapping, LOMN template build, RTM configuration. Week 2: staff training (by role — coordinators, schedulers, providers) and parallel testing. Week 3: live with clinIQ support available for adjustments.

cta.headline: See how neurosurgery practices capture post-op RTM and cut LOMN time to 5 minutes
cta.subhead: 15-minute demo. Case coordination pipeline, LOMN template library, post-op RTM dashboard, OR preparation checklist.

testimonial.quote: We were spending 45 minutes per LOMN and leaving post-op RTM on the table completely. clinIQ paid for itself in the first month of RTM billing alone.
